content:
# Revelation Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 289-290 | **Source Image:** REV_017_page_001.jpg, REV_017_page_002.jpg

1. And one from the seven angels, the [ones] having the seven bowls, came and spoke with me, saying, Come, I will show to you the judgment of the great harlot, the [one] sitting upon many waters,

2. with whom the kings of the earth committed fornication, and the [ones] dwelling upon the earth were made drunk from the wine of the fornication of her.

3. And he carried me away into a wilderness in [the] Spirit. And I saw a woman sitting upon a scarlet beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns.

4. And the woman was clothed in purple and scarlet, and having been gilded with gold and precious stone and pearls, having a golden cup in the hand of her, full of abominations and the unclean [things] of the fornication of her,

5. and upon the forehead of her a name having been written, a mystery: BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF THE HARLOTS AND OF THE ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH.

6. And I saw the woman being drunk from the blood of the holy [ones] and from the blood of the witnesses of Yeshua. And I marveled, having seen her, [with] great amazement.

7. And the angel said to me, Why did you marvel? I will tell to you the mystery of the woman and of the beast, the [one] carrying her, the [one] having the seven heads and the ten horns.

8. The beast which you saw was, and is not, and is about to come up from the abyss and to go into destruction. And the [ones] dwelling upon the earth will marvel — [those] whose name has not been written upon the scroll of the life from [the] foundation of [the] world — seeing the beast, that it was, and is not, and will be present.

9. Here [is] the mind, the [one] having wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, where the woman sits upon them.

10. And they are seven kings; the five fell, the one is, the other has not yet come; and when he comes, he must remain a little [while].

11. And the beast which was, and is not, he also is an eighth, and is from the seven, and goes into destruction.

12. And the ten horns which you saw are ten kings, who have not yet received a kingdom, but they receive authority as kings [for] one hour with the beast.

13. These have one mind, and the power and authority of them they give to the beast.

14. These will make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb will conquer them, because Lord of lords he is and King of kings — and the [ones] with him, called and chosen and faithful.

15. And he says to me, The waters which you saw, where the harlot sits, are peoples and multitudes and nations and tongues.

16. And the ten horns which you saw, and the beast, these will hate the harlot, and having been made desolate they will make her, and naked, and the flesh of her they will eat, and her they will burn with fire.

17. For the God gave into the hearts of them to do the purpose of him, and to do one purpose, and to give the kingdom of them to the beast, until the words of the God will be completed.

18. And the woman whom you saw is the great city, the [one] having a kingdom over the kings of the earth.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **17:1** — Sinaiticus reads "one from" (εις εκ) the seven angels, consistent with the pattern in 21:9.

2. **17:4** — Sinaiticus reads "having been gilded" (κεχρυσωμενη), a perfect passive participle of χρυσοω, reflecting the ornate description literally.

3. **17:5** — The word "mystery" (μυστηριον) appears as part of the inscription itself in Sinaiticus. Some later manuscripts take it as a descriptor ("a name written, a mystery:") rather than part of the title. EMET follows the Sinaiticus reading where it stands at the beginning of the inscription.

4. **17:8** — Sinaiticus reads "will be present" (παρεσται) rather than the Byzantine "and yet is" (καιπερ εστιν). This is a significant variant; the Sinaiticus reading emphasizes future presence rather than paradoxical present existence.

5. **17:9** — "Here [is] the mind, the [one] having wisdom" — Sinaiticus uses ωδε ο νους ο εχων σοφιαν. This is rendered hyper-literally; the sense is "here is [what requires] the mind having wisdom."

6. **17:16** — "having been made desolate they will make her" preserves the awkward Greek construction (ηρημωμενην ποιησουσιν αυτην), where the perfect participle precedes the main verb. The sense is "they will make her desolate."

7. **17:8** — Sinaiticus has "name" (ονομα) in the singular, not "names" (ονοματα) as in some manuscripts, for those written in the book of life.

— EMET
